title: COPD — Stable
oneLiner: Progressive, preventable lung disease – chronic airflow limitation from smoking/biomass exposure – managed with inhalers, smoking cessation, pulmonary rehab.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute exacerbation** – single finding: sudden increase in dyspnoea, sputum purulence, or sputum volume. **Must differentiate from stable worsening** – use ABG / CXR.
- **Pneumothorax** – single finding: acute pleuritic pain + hyperresonance + sudden oxygen desaturation; bedside lung ultrasound or CXR confirms.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – single finding: unilateral leg swelling + tachycardia + acute dyspnoea not relieved by bronchodilators; use PERC/Wells + D-dimer.
- **Myocardial infarction / acute heart failure** – single finding: chest tightness + raised troponin / BNP + signs of pulmonary oedema on CXR.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive dyspnoea (worse on exertion)
  - Chronic cough (often morning) with sputum
  - Wheeze / chest tightness
  - Reduced exercise tolerance
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Barrel chest, pursed-lip breathing
  - Reduced breath sounds, wheeze, prolonged expiration
  - Hyperinflation (tracheal tug, lower rib cage indrawing)
  - Signs of right heart failure (cor pulmonale): peripheral oedema, raised JVP, hepatomegaly – if severe/late
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Asthma** – variable symptoms, often no fixed obstruction, normal between attacks, more common in younger atopy. **Splitter:** reversibility testing (FEV1 improves by >12% and >200mL after bronchodilator suggests asthma, not COPD).
- **Heart failure** – bibasal crackles, S3, raised BNP, cardiomegaly on CXR.
- **Bronchiectasis** – copious purulent sputum, crackles, finger clubbing, recurrent infections.
- **Interstitial lung disease** – dry cough, fine inspiratory crackles, restrictive pattern on spirometry.
- **Lung cancer** – focal signs (effusion, mass), weight loss, haemoptysis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Spirometry (pre- and post-bronchodilator) – **mandatory for diagnosis** – FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms airflow obstruction.
  - Pulse oximetry – if <92% on room air do ABG.
  - Peak flow – not diagnostic, useful for monitoring.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, polycythaemia), U&Es (monitor if diuretics), CRP (rule out infection).
  - Alpha-1 antitrypsin if onset <45y or minimal smoking history.
  - BNP (if suspect heart failure).
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR – rule out other pathology; signs: hyperinflation, flat diaphragm, bullae.
  - CT chest – if surgery/volume reduction considered, or to exclude malignancy.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **NICE/BTS:** post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 + appropriate symptoms + smoking history (or biomass exposure).
  - **GOLD staging of severity:** FEV1% predicted → GOLD 1 (≥80%), 2 (50-79%), 3 (30-49%), 4 (<30%).
- **Never-miss test:** **Spirometry** – omission leads to misdiagnosis and wrong treatment.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Establish if stable (no exacerbation) → **no emergency intervention**.
  - If acute-on-chronic deterioration → treat as exacerbation (see separate entry).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Review inhaler technique – **commonest cause of poor control**.
  - Ensure smoking cessation advice + offer nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) or varenicline.
  - Optimise inhaled therapy per symptom burden and exacerbation history:
    - Mild dyspnoea ± single exacerbation/year → SABA prn (e.g. salbutamol)
    - Moderate dyspnoea or ≥2 exacerbations/year → LABA+LAMA (e.g. indacaterol/glycopyrronium)
    - Severe dyspnoea or asthma features → LABA+LAMA+ICS (e.g. beclometasone/formoterol/glycopyrronium)
  - Start pulmonary rehabilitation (if eligible – FEV1 ≤50% predicted or ≥1 exacerbation/year).
  - Vaccinate: **annual influenza**, **pneumococcal** (once).
  - Check oxygen saturation on room air – prescribe ambulatory oxygen if saturations <90% on exercise.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - 6-monthly spirometry + symptom review.
  - Refer for home oxygen assessment if PaO2 <7.3kPa (or <8kPa with cor pulmonale).
  - Consider lung volume reduction surgery / bronchoscopic valves (if emphysema, hyperinflation).
  - Palliative care referral if severe disease (MRC breathlessness score 4-5).
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):**
  - Continue usual inhalers.
  - VTE prophylaxis – standard (enoxaparin unless contraindicated).
  - O2 target 88-92% – avoid hyperoxia.
  - Observations: baseline, then 6-12 hourly if stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - SABA (salbutamol) 100-200mcg via MDI or nebulised 2.5-5mg PRN.
  - LAMA (tiotropium) 18mcg inhalation capsule daily (HandiHaler) or 2.5mcg daily (Respimat).
  - LABA (salmeterol) 50mcg bd.
  - ICS (beclometasone) 100-400mcg bd via MDI. Use in combination only if eosinophilic phenotype (blood eosinophils >300 cells/µL) or coexistent asthma.
  - Triple therapy (e.g., Trelegy Ellipta 92/55/22mcg once daily) – for frequent exacerbations on dual therapy.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ipratropium (SAMA) 20-40mcg PRN (if unable to tolerate tiotropium).
  - Theophylline 200-400mg od (modified-release) – third-line; check levels (target 10-20 mg/L).
  - Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 30mg od for 5 days) only for acute exacerbations, not routine stable use.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - LABA + β-blockers (non-selective) – avoid if possible (increase bronchospasm).
  - Theophylline → check levels with ciprofloxacin, macrolides, oral contraceptives.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Tiotropium – no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment (but caution if severe CrCl <30).
  - Theophylline – reduce dose in heart failure, liver disease, elderly.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Inhaled corticosteroid discontinuation in eosinophilic COPD** – sudden ICS withdrawal can trigger exacerbations; never stop ICS without review. Also, **excessive oxygen (hyperoxia)** → CO2 retention → respiratory acidosis → death.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Exacerbations (infective, non-infective)
  - Pneumonia, pneumothorax
  - Respiratory failure (type I or II)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (AF, SVT)
- **Chronic:**
  - Pulmonary hypertension → cor pulmonale (peripheral oedema, raised JVP)
  - Cachexia, muscle wasting
  - Depression/anxiety (screen routinely)
  - Osteoporosis (especially if on frequent steroids)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** 30-day mortality after admission for exacerbation ~5-10%; 5-year survival ~50-60% for GOLD stage 3-4.
- **Chronicity:** Progressive but modifiable – smoking cessation slows decline; pulmonary rehab improves QoL and reduces admissions.
- **BODE index** (BMI, airflow obstruction, dyspnoea, exercise capacity) – predicts mortality better than FEV1 alone.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do** check blood eosinophils before switching ICS – eosinophil count guides ICS response.
- **Do** refer all eligible patients for pulmonary rehab – it works better than most drugs for symptoms.
- **Do** use the **Medical Research Council (MRC) Dyspnoea Scale** to quantify breathlessness and guide therapy escalation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not** withhold long-acting bronchodilators because of "mild" disease – patients with few symptoms still benefit from LAMA if >1 exacerbation/year.
- **Do not** prescribe home oxygen for patients who continue smoking – risk of fire/fatal burn.
- **Do not** use ICS alone (without LABA) – monotherapy ineffective in COPD, increases pneumonia risk.
- **Do not** discharge without checking **inhaler technique** – most "uncontrolled" COPD is actually poor inhaler use.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening breathlessness not relieved by usual inhalers
  - Fever >38°C or new purulent sputum (signs of exacerbation)
  - Chest pain, sudden worsening, or confusion
- **Follow-up:** GP within 2 weeks; specialist respiratory review if ≥2 exacerbations/year or FEV1 <50%.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEW2 score ≥5 with respiratory rate >25 or SpO2 <88% despite oxygen
  - pH <7.35 (respiratory acidosis) → consider NIV/ICU
  - GCS <15 or worsening confusion
  - Haemodynamic instability (MAP <65, lactate >2)
- **Threshold for NIV:** pH 7.25-7.35 with PaCO2 >6.0 kPa and respiratory acidosis after optimal medical therapy.

rawMarkdown:
## COPD — Stable
**One-liner:** Progressive, preventable lung disease – chronic airflow limitation from smoking/biomass exposure – managed with inhalers, smoking cessation, pulmonary rehab.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute exacerbation** – single finding: sudden increase in dyspnoea, sputum purulence, or sputum volume. **Must differentiate from stable worsening** – use ABG / CXR.
- **Pneumothorax** – single finding: acute pleuritic pain + hyperresonance + sudden oxygen desaturation; bedside lung ultrasound or CXR confirms.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – single finding: unilateral leg swelling + tachycardia + acute dyspnoea not relieved by bronchodilators; use PERC/Wells + D-dimer.
- **Myocardial infarction / acute heart failure** – single finding: chest tightness + raised troponin / BNP + signs of pulmonary oedema on CXR.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive dyspnoea (worse on exertion)
  - Chronic cough (often morning) with sputum
  - Wheeze / chest tightness
  - Reduced exercise tolerance
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Barrel chest, pursed-lip breathing
  - Reduced breath sounds, wheeze, prolonged expiration
  - Hyperinflation (tracheal tug, lower rib cage indrawing)
  - Signs of right heart failure (cor pulmonale): peripheral oedema, raised JVP, hepatomegaly – if severe/late

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Asthma** – variable symptoms, often no fixed obstruction, normal between attacks, more common in younger atopy. **Splitter:** reversibility testing (FEV1 improves by >12% and >200mL after bronchodilator suggests asthma, not COPD).
- **Heart failure** – bibasal crackles, S3, raised BNP, cardiomegaly on CXR.
- **Bronchiectasis** – copious purulent sputum, crackles, finger clubbing, recurrent infections.
- **Interstitial lung disease** – dry cough, fine inspiratory crackles, restrictive pattern on spirometry.
- **Lung cancer** – focal signs (effusion, mass), weight loss, haemoptysis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Spirometry (pre- and post-bronchodilator) – **mandatory for diagnosis** – FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms airflow obstruction.
  - Pulse oximetry – if <92% on room air do ABG.
  - Peak flow – not diagnostic, useful for monitoring.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, polycythaemia), U&Es (monitor if diuretics), CRP (rule out infection).
  - Alpha-1 antitrypsin if onset <45y or minimal smoking history.
  - BNP (if suspect heart failure).
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR – rule out other pathology; signs: hyperinflation, flat diaphragm, bullae.
  - CT chest – if surgery/volume reduction considered, or to exclude malignancy.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **NICE/BTS:** post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 + appropriate symptoms + smoking history (or biomass exposure).
  - **GOLD staging of severity:** FEV1% predicted → GOLD 1 (≥80%), 2 (50-79%), 3 (30-49%), 4 (<30%).
- **Never-miss test:** **Spirometry** – omission leads to misdiagnosis and wrong treatment.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Establish if stable (no exacerbation) → **no emergency intervention**.
  - If acute-on-chronic deterioration → treat as exacerbation (see separate entry).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Review inhaler technique – **commonest cause of poor control**.
  - Ensure smoking cessation advice + offer nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) or varenicline.
  - Optimise inhaled therapy per symptom burden and exacerbation history:
    - Mild dyspnoea ± single exacerbation/year → SABA prn (e.g. salbutamol)
    - Moderate dyspnoea or ≥2 exacerbations/year → LABA+LAMA (e.g. indacaterol/glycopyrronium)
    - Severe dyspnoea or asthma features → LABA+LAMA+ICS (e.g. beclometasone/formoterol/glycopyrronium)
  - Start pulmonary rehabilitation (if eligible – FEV1 ≤50% predicted or ≥1 exacerbation/year).
  - Vaccinate: **annual influenza**, **pneumococcal** (once).
  - Check oxygen saturation on room air – prescribe ambulatory oxygen if saturations <90% on exercise.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - 6-monthly spirometry + symptom review.
  - Refer for home oxygen assessment if PaO2 <7.3kPa (or <8kPa with cor pulmonale).
  - Consider lung volume reduction surgery / bronchoscopic valves (if emphysema, hyperinflation).
  - Palliative care referral if severe disease (MRC breathlessness score 4-5).
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):**
  - Continue usual inhalers.
  - VTE prophylaxis – standard (enoxaparin unless contraindicated).
  - O2 target 88-92% – avoid hyperoxia.
  - Observations: baseline, then 6-12 hourly if stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - SABA (salbutamol) 100-200mcg via MDI or nebulised 2.5-5mg PRN.
  - LAMA (tiotropium) 18mcg inhalation capsule daily (HandiHaler) or 2.5mcg daily (Respimat).
  - LABA (salmeterol) 50mcg bd.
  - ICS (beclometasone) 100-400mcg bd via MDI. Use in combination only if eosinophilic phenotype (blood eosinophils >300 cells/µL) or coexistent asthma.
  - Triple therapy (e.g., Trelegy Ellipta 92/55/22mcg once daily) – for frequent exacerbations on dual therapy.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ipratropium (SAMA) 20-40mcg PRN (if unable to tolerate tiotropium).
  - Theophylline 200-400mg od (modified-release) – third-line; check levels (target 10-20 mg/L).
  - Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 30mg od for 5 days) only for acute exacerbations, not routine stable use.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - LABA + β-blockers (non-selective) – avoid if possible (increase bronchospasm).
  - Theophylline → check levels with ciprofloxacin, macrolides, oral contraceptives.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Tiotropium – no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment (but caution if severe CrCl <30).
  - Theophylline – reduce dose in heart failure, liver disease, elderly.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Inhaled corticosteroid discontinuation in eosinophilic COPD** – sudden ICS withdrawal can trigger exacerbations; never stop ICS without review. Also, **excessive oxygen (hyperoxia)** → CO2 retention → respiratory acidosis → death.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Exacerbations (infective, non-infective)
  - Pneumonia, pneumothorax
  - Respiratory failure (type I or II)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (AF, SVT)
- **Chronic:**
  - Pulmonary hypertension → cor pulmonale (peripheral oedema, raised JVP)
  - Cachexia, muscle wasting
  - Depression/anxiety (screen routinely)
  - Osteoporosis (especially if on frequent steroids)

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** 30-day mortality after admission for exacerbation ~5-10%; 5-year survival ~50-60% for GOLD stage 3-4.
- **Chronicity:** Progressive but modifiable – smoking cessation slows decline; pulmonary rehab improves QoL and reduces admissions.
- **BODE index** (BMI, airflow obstruction, dyspnoea, exercise capacity) – predicts mortality better than FEV1 alone.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do** check blood eosinophils before switching ICS – eosinophil count guides ICS response.
- **Do** refer all eligible patients for pulmonary rehab – it works better than most drugs for symptoms.
- **Do** use the **Medical Research Council (MRC) Dyspnoea Scale** to quantify breathlessness and guide therapy escalation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not** withhold long-acting bronchodilators because of "mild" disease – patients with few symptoms still benefit from LAMA if >1 exacerbation/year.
- **Do not** prescribe home oxygen for patients who continue smoking – risk of fire/fatal burn.
- **Do not** use ICS alone (without LABA) – monotherapy ineffective in COPD, increases pneumonia risk.
- **Do not** discharge without checking **inhaler technique** – most "uncontrolled" COPD is actually poor inhaler use.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening breathlessness not relieved by usual inhalers
  - Fever >38°C or new purulent sputum (signs of exacerbation)
  - Chest pain, sudden worsening, or confusion
- **Follow-up:** GP within 2 weeks; specialist respiratory review if ≥2 exacerbations/year or FEV1 <50%.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEW2 score ≥5 with respiratory rate >25 or SpO2 <88% despite oxygen
  - pH <7.35 (respiratory acidosis) → consider NIV/ICU
  - GCS <15 or worsening confusion
  - Haemodynamic instability (MAP <65, lactate >2)
- **Threshold for NIV:** pH 7.25-7.35 with PaCO2 >6.0 kPa and respiratory acidosis after optimal medical therapy.
